# Experimental Design {#sec-experimental-design}

## Why design experiments?

Experimental design is crucial in animal research, and has two primary goals:

1. maximise the amount of information gained
2. reduce the number of animals required

::: { .callout-tip }
Carefully-planned experimental designs have an advantage in that they can greatly simplify downstream (statistical) analysis.
:::

::: { .callout-note title="Practical benefits of experimental design" }
Appropriate experimental design can give us flexibility in conducting an experiment

Taking account factors such as which technician did the work, day/time of experiment, which equivalent piece of equipment was used, and so on we can safely conduct an experiment over a longer time period and more conveniently. Doing so also allows us to estimate any influence of these factors over the experimental outcome, if it exists.
:::

::: { .callout-caution title="Statistical benefits of experimental design" }
Good experimental design aims to increase the ratio of _signal_ (the effect you are looking to measure) to _noise_ (the background variability in measurements).

1. **Reducing variability**: Suppose that animal behaviour varies between Mondays (after the quiet of a weekend), and other weekdays. By including a factor in the experiment of `Monday` vs `not Monday`, or by treating each day individually in the experimental design, we could account for variability in animal response between days of the week.
2. **Increasing signal**: If we can ethically ensure a greater variation between _treatment_ and _control_, then this can help reduce the number of individuals required. For example, if one strain of mouse produces a stronger inflammatory response than another, then selecting the strain with the stronger response may require fewer mice to detect a meaningful effect due to intervention. It may even be worthwhile running pilot studies to identify such differences.
3. **Reducing bias**: We are human and suffer from unconscious biases, such as wanting - subconsciously - to see our research work as we think it should. This can lead to unintentional differences in measurement, if we know which subjects receive treatment and which do not. _Blinding_ and _randomisation_ can help to eliminate such bias
:::
